concepts.title: Some Basic Definitions
concepts.conceptBody:
Some basic definitions

Experiment : An experiment is an operation which can result in more than one way.

Random Experiment : An experiment whose outcome can not be predicted with certainty is called a random experiment.

Examples : (i) "Throwing an unbiased die" is a random experiment because when a die is thrown we cannot say with certainty which one of the numbers $1,2,3,4,5$ and 6 will come up.

(ii) "Tossing of a fair coin" is a random experiment because when a coin is tossed we cannot say with certainty whether either a head or a tail will come up.

(iii) "Drawing a card from a well-shuffled pack of cards" is a random experiment.

Note : (i) A pack of cards consists of 52 cards in 4 suits i.e., 
(a) Spades (b) Clubs 
(c) Hearts (d) Diamonds.

Each suit consists of 13 cards. Out of these, spades and clubs are black faced cards, while hearts and diamonds are red-faced cards. The aces, kings, queens, Jack (or knave) are called face cards or honor cards.

Sample Space 

The set of all possible results of a random experiment is called the sample space of that experiment and it is generally denoted by $\text{S}$.

Examples: (i) If we toss a coin, there are two possible results, namely a head (H) or

Or a tail (T).

So, the sample space in this experiment is given by

$\text{S}=\left\{ \text{H},\text{T} \right\}$.

(ii) When two coins are tossed, the sample space

$\text{S}=\left\{ \text{HH},\text{HT},\text{TH},\text{TT} \right\}$

where $\text{HH}$ denotes the head on the first coin and head on the second coin, Similarly HT denotes the head on the first coin and tail on the second coin.

concepts.title: Probability of occurrence of an event
concepts.conceptBody:
Probability of occurrence of an event

Let $\text{S}$ be the sample space, then the probability of occurrence of an event $\text{E}$ is denoted by $\text{P}\left( \text{E} \right)$ and is defined as

$P\left( E \right)$ $=\frac{n\left( E \right)}{n\left( S \right)}=\frac{\text{ number }\text{ of }\text{ elements }\text{ in }E}{\text{ number }\text{ of }\text{ elements }\text{ in }S}$

$=\frac{\text{ number }\text{ of }\text{ cases }\text{ favourable }\text{ to }\text{ event }E}{\text{ total }\text{ number }\text{ of }\text{ cases }}$

Event 

(i) When a coin is tossed, sample space $\text{S}=\left\{ \text{H},\text{T} \right\}$

Let $\text{E}=$ the event of occurrence of a head.

Then $\text{E}=\left\{ \text{H} \right\}$

$\therefore \text{P}\left( \text{E} \right)=$ Probability of occurrence of head $=\frac{\text{n}\left( \text{E} \right)}{\text{n}\left( \text{S} \right)}=\frac{1}{2}$

(ii) When a die is tossed, sample space $\text{S}=\left\{ 1,2,3,4,5,6 \right\}$

Let $\text{A}=$ the event of occurrence of an odd number $=\left\{ 1,3,5 \right\}$

$\text{B}=$ the event of occurrence of a number greater than $4=\left\{ 5,6 \right\}$.

Then $\text{P}\left( \text{A} \right)=\frac{\text{n}\left( \text{A} \right)}{\text{n}\left( \text{S} \right)}=\frac{3}{6}=\frac{1}{2}$

And $\text{P}\left( \text{B} \right)=\frac{\text{n}\left( \text{B} \right)}{\text{n}\left( \text{S} \right)}=\frac{2}{6}=\frac{1}{3}$

(ii) When one ball is drawn at random from a bag containing 3 black and 4 red balls (balls of the same colour being identical or different), then sample space $S=\left\{ {{B}_{1}},{{B}_{2}},{{B}_{3}},{{R}_{1}},{{R}_{2}},{{R}_{3}},{{R}_{4}} \right\}$.

$\therefore \text{ n}\left( \text{S} \right)=7$

Here the three black balls may be denoted by ${{\text{B}}_{1}},\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{B}}_{2}}$ and ${{\text{B}}_{3}}$ even if they are identical because while finding probability only number of black and red balls are to be taken into account.

Let $\text{E}=$ the event of occurrence of a red ball.

Then $\text{E}=\left\{ {{\text{R}}_{1}},{{\text{R}}_{2}},{{\text{R}}_{3}},{{\text{R}}_{4}} \right\}\therefore \text{n}\left( \text{E} \right)=4$

Now $\text{P}\left( \text{E} \right)=\frac{\text{n}\left( \text{E} \right)}{\text{n}\left( \text{S} \right)}=\frac{4}{7}$

(iii) When two coins are tossed, sample space $\text{S}=\left\{ \text{HH},\text{HT},\text{TH},\text{TT} \right\}$.

Let $\text{E}=$ the event of occurrence of one head and one tail, then

$\text{E}=\left\{ \text{HT},\text{TH} \right\}$

Now $\text{P}\left( \text{E} \right)=\frac{\text{n}\left( \text{E} \right)}{\text{n}\left( \text{S} \right)}=\frac{2}{4}=\frac{1}{2}$

Theorem 1.: Probability of occurrence of an event is a number lying between 0 and 1. 

$0\le \mathbf{P}\left( \mathbf{E} \right)\le 1$ 

Note : (i) If $\phi $ is the impossible event, thenP $\left( \phi \right)\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }=\frac{\text{n}\left( \phi \right)}{\text{n}\left( \text{S} \right)}=\frac{0}{\text{n}\left( \text{S} \right)}=0$

(ii) If $\text{S}$ be the sure event, then $\text{P}\left( \text{S} \right)=\frac{\text{n}\left( \text{S} \right)}{\text{n}\left( \text{S} \right)}=1$.

(iii) $\text{P}\left( \text{E} \right)=0\Leftrightarrow \text{E}=\phi $ and $\text{P}\left( \text{E} \right)=1\Leftrightarrow \text{E}=\text{S}$

Theorem 2. : If is any event and ${E}'$ be the complement of event E , then $P\left( E \right)+P\left( {{E}'} \right)=1$. 

Odds in Favour and Odds Against an Event: 

Let $\text{S}$ be the sample space and $\text{E}$ be an event. Let $\text{{E}'}$ denote the complement of event $\text{E}$, then

(i) odds in favour of even $\text{E}$

$=\frac{\text{n}\left( \text{E} \right)}{\text{n}\left( {\text{{E}'}} \right)}=\frac{\text{ number }\text{ of }\text{ cases }\text{ favourable }\text{ to }\text{ event }\text{ E}}{\text{ number }\text{ of }\text{ cases }\text{ against }\text{ E}}$

(ii) odds against an event $\text{E}$

$=\frac{n\left( {{E}'} \right)}{n\left( E \right)}=\frac{\text{ number }\text{ of }\text{ cases }\text{ against }\text{ the }\text{ event }E}{\text{ number }\text{ of }\text{ cases }\text{ favourable }\text{ to }\text{ event }E}$

From definitions given above it is clear that,

Note : (i) Odds in favour of event $\text{E}$

$=\frac{\text{n}\left( \text{E} \right)}{\text{n}\left( {\text{{E}'}} \right)}=\frac{\text{n}\left( \text{E} \right)/\text{n}\left( \text{S} \right)}{\text{n}\left( {\text{{E}'}} \right)/\text{n}\left( \text{S} \right)}=\frac{\text{P}\left( \text{E} \right)}{\text{P}\left( {\text{{E}'}} \right)}$

and odds against event $E=\frac{n\left( {{E}'} \right)}{n\left( E \right)}=\frac{P\left( {{E}'} \right)}{P\left( E \right)}$

(ii) If any one of $\text{P}\left( \text{E} \right)$, odds in favour of $\text{E}$ and odds against $\text{E}$ is given, then other two can be determined.

If odds in favour of an event are $a:b$ then the probability of the occurrence of that event is $a/\left( a+b \right)$ and of non-occurrence is $b/\left( a+b \right)$.

Ex. (i) If $\text{P}\left( \text{E} \right)=\frac{2}{7}$, then odds in favour of $\text{E}=\frac{2}{5}$ and odds against $\text{E}=\frac{5}{2}$.

(ii) If odds against $\text{E}=\frac{3}{11}$, then odds in favour of $\text{E}=\frac{11}{3}$ and $\text{P}\left( \text{E} \right)=\frac{11}{14}$

(iii) If odds in favour of $\text{E}=\frac{3}{8}$, then odds against $\text{E}=\frac{8}{3}$ and $\text{P}\left( \text{E} \right)=\frac{3}{11}$

Illustration 1 

If two dice are thrown simultaneously, represent the sample space and the following events. 

(i) the sum of the numbers coming up is greater than 9. 

(ii) The sum of the numbers coming up is 8. 

Solution: 

Here random experiment is: Throwing of two dice simultaneously.

Let $\text{S}=$ the sample space

$\text{A}=$ the event that the sum of the numbers coming up is not less than 9.

and $\text{B}=$ the event that the sum of the numbers coming up is 8.

Let $1,2,3,4,5$ and 6 denote the occurrence of numbers $1,2,3,4,5,6$ respectively on a die. Let $\left( m,n \right)$ denote the occurrence of $m$ on first die and $n$ on second die, then

S =$~\{\left( 1,1 \right),\left( 1,2 \right),\left( 1,3 \right),\left( 1,4 \right),\left( 1,5 \right),\left( 1,6 \right),\left( 2,1 \right),\left( 2,2 \right),\left( 2,3 \right),\left( 2,4 \right),\left( 2,5 \right),\left( 2,6 \right),\left( 3,1 \right),$

$~\left( 3,2 \right),\left( 3,3 \right),\left( 3,4 \right),\left( 3,5 \right),\left( 3,6 \right),\left( 4,1 \right),\left( 4,2 \right),\left( 4,3 \right),\left( 4,4 \right),\left( 4,5 \right),\left( 4,6 \right),\left( 5,1 \right),\left( 5,2 \right),$

$\left( 5,3 \right),\left( 5,4 \right),\left( 5,5 \right),\left( 5,6 \right),\left( 6,1 \right),\left( 6,2 \right),\left( 6,3 \right),\left( 6,4 \right),\left( 6,5 \right),\left( 6,6 \right)\}$

$\text{A}=\left\{ \left( 6,3 \right),\left( 5,4 \right),\left( 4,5 \right),\left( 3,6 \right),\left( 6,4 \right),\left( 5,5 \right),\left( 4,6 \right),\left( 6,5 \right),\left( 5,6 \right),\left( 6,6 \right) \right\}$

and $\text{B}=\left\{ \left( 6,2 \right),\left( 5,3 \right),\left( 4,4 \right),\left( 3,5 \right),\left( 2,6 \right) \right\}$

Illustration 2 

If three coins are tossed, represent the sample space and the event of getting two heads and one tail and also find the number of elements in them. 

Solution: 

Here random experiment is: tossing of three coins.

Let $\text{S}=$ the sample space

and $\text{E}=$ the event of occurrence of two heads and one tail.

Let $\text{H}$ denote the occurrence of head and $\text{T}$ the occurrence of tail when one coin is tossed.

Then $\text{S}=$ $~\left\{ \text{H},\text{T} \right\}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\left\{ \text{H},\text{T} \right\}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\left\{ \text{H},\text{T} \right\}$

=$~\{\left( \text{H},\text{H},\text{H} \right),\left( \text{H},\text{H},\text{T} \right),\left( \text{H},\text{T},\text{H} \right),\left( \text{T},\text{H},\text{H} \right),$

$~\left( \text{H},\text{T},\text{T} \right),\left( \text{T},\text{H},\text{T} \right),\left( \text{T},\text{T},\text{H} \right),\left( \text{T},\text{T},\text{T} \right)\}$

And $\text{E}=\left\{ \left( \text{H},\text{H},\text{T} \right),\left( \text{H},\text{T},\text{H} \right),\left( \text{T},\text{H},\text{H} \right) \right\}$

Also $\text{n}\left( \text{S} \right)=8\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ and }\!\!~\!\!\text{ n}\left( \text{E} \right)=3.\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$

Illustration 3 

A coin is tossed successively three times. Find the probability of getting exactly one head or two heads. 

Solution: 

Let $\text{S}$ be the sample space and $\text{E}$ be the event of getting exactly one head or exactly two heads, then

And $\text{S}=\left\{ \text{HHH},\text{HHT},\text{HTH},\text{THH},\text{TTH},\text{THT},\text{HTT},\text{TTT} \right\}.$

$\text{E}=\left\{ \text{HHT},\text{HTH},\text{THH},\text{HTT},\text{THT},\text{TTH} \right\}$

$\therefore $ $\text{n}\left( \text{E} \right)=6\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ and }\!\!~\!\!\text{ n}\left( \text{S} \right)=8.\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$

Now required probability, $\text{P}\left( \text{E} \right)=\frac{\text{n}\left( \text{E} \right)}{\text{n}\left( \text{S} \right)}=\frac{6}{8}=\frac{3}{4}$
